chunk_summary: This section focuses on culturally adapted meal planning and age-based meal scheduling, emphasizing structured meal architecture, nutrient density, and cultural sensitivity for effective nutrition counselling across different age groups.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific meal architecture and scheduling for infants, toddlers, school-age children, and adolescents.
Learn culturally sensitive strategies for meal planning, including staple-based templates and household measures.
Identify methods to address undernutrition and growth faltering using energy-dense and nutrient-rich foods.
Incorporate protein and iron bioavailability strategies into culturally adapted meal plans.
key_facts: Infants 6–8 months require 2–3 thick complementary feeds daily alongside breastmilk.
Toddlers benefit from 3 meals and 2 planned snacks with minimal grazing.
School-age children need breakfast as a non-negotiable anchor and a planned tiffin.
Vegetarian families should combine cereals and pulses (e.g., roti with dal) for complete protein.
Iron bioavailability improves when paired with vitamin C sources and separated from tea consumption.
For undernutrition, prioritize thicker consistency foods, added oil/ghee, and nutrient-dense snacks over dilute options.
reference_quote: Infants 6–8 months need thick complementary foods 2–3 times daily alongside breastmilk; 9–11 months need 3–4 feeds plus 1–2 nutritious snacks with texture progression. (IAP Infant and Young Child Feeding guidelines, 2016)
